dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a confirmed shooting incident at 23rd and Broad.
Hi, I'm Ariya, an EMT with the rescue squad. We received a call about a shooting. Can you confirm what you saw?
There was a man in the street with a head wound. He's unresponsive and bleeding heavily from the head and side.
He's not moving or responding. I don't think he's breathing properly.
GCS is 3 - no eye opening, no verbal or motor response. He is in altered mental status.
No eye opening, verbal response, or motor reaction to any stimulus. Fully unresponsive.
I'll assess the airway. Positioning now. I'll check for obstruction and patency.
Inserting OPA for airway control. No resistance. Gag reflex absent.
Breathing is agonal and ineffective. Rate is 8 breaths per minute.
Pulse is 92. Initial blood pressure is 144/59. Oxygen saturation is 59%.
Skin is diaphoretic and warm. A wound is in the left axilla; two holes to the head with brain matter visible.
Pupils are non-reactive at 8mm. No response to light or other stimuli.
Breath sounds reduced on the left. Midline trachea. No rales, wheezing, or rhonchi.
Pulse is 92 and regular at this moment.
Respiratory rate remains 8 with ineffective respirations.
Blood pressure recorded at 144/59.
Oxygen saturation is at 59% on room air.
4-lead EKG showing sinus rhythm with ST elevation, suggesting cardiac involvement.
Blood glucose is at 0.
Suggestive findings of subdural hemorrhage given non-reactive pupils and AMS.
Begin fluid resuscitation. First attempt with IV in an extremity vein.
IV attempt unsuccessful; proceeding to intraosseous access.
IO access successfully placed in the left humeral head.
Pushing normal saline through the IO, volume approximately 150 mL.
Cardiac pads applied for monitoring. Ready for any in-transit change.
Capnography applied via nasal cannula, later switched to inline with BVM.
Following standard trauma protocol, suspecting cranial and cardiac injuries.
Pulse now at 89, respiratory rate 12 with worsening perfusion. BP is 62/54.
Pulse stable at 89, respiratory rate 12. Breathing continues to worsen.
No evidence of pain - patient is pain level 0.
Transporting to VCU Health Systems due to patient's critical condition.
Patient secured on stretcher with head and neck immobilized. RFD assistance used.
Hot handoff expected at emergency department.
On arrival, patient became hypotensive and unresponsive.
Intubation deferred in the field; airway maintained with BVM and OPA.
Arrest occurred after handoff; pronounced dead in the ED.
Patient's clothes, shoes, and phone were left at the bedside.
AMS confirmed with no eye opening, fixed, and non-reactive pupils.
OPA placed without resistance; airway remains clear. Gag reflex absent.
Capnography waveform present with BVM; no effective ventilation detected.
Pupils 8mm, fixed and non-reactive - indicative of intracranial trauma.
No clear ear or nasal drainage. Airway patent and no obstruction.
Breath sounds asymmetric, no tracheal deviation or pneumothorax signs.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with ST segment elevation; treat as cardiac event.
ABD soft, non-tender, slightly distended with incontinence.
No external urological trauma observed.
No notable deformity, only multiple GSWs noted to head and axilla.
Glucose remains at 0; no further testing conducted.
Continuous normal saline via IO. Pressure bag active.
Cardiopulmonary pads used for monitoring through transport.
IO site stable, infusions ongoing. No fluid leak or swelling observed.
Trauma management protocol followed throughout based on severe injuries.
Pulse at 89 with 12 respiratory rate. BP and Spo2 continue to decline.
IO infusions continue. No new interventions required.
No evidence of pain; patient is unresponsive.
No medical history gathered due to AMS and patient unresponsiveness.
Patient secured and immobilized; continuous airway monitoring.
BVM providing ventilation; airway free of secretions.
Capnography remains flat despite BVM use.
IO infusions ongoing. Estimated volume 150 mL administered.
Patient shows declining vitals and waveform capnography - prepare for arrest.
Primary concerns remain GSWs to head and possible cardiac trauma.
Transporting under Code status. ED alerted of critical condition.
Hot handoff planned. ED aware of full clinical status.
Male, approximately 18. No known ID. GSWs to head area; no stab wounds observed.
Resuscitation performed via IO due to IV failure.
No movement or response since assessment; GCS remains at 3.
Airway maintained via BVM and OPA. No signs of airway resistance.
Blood pressure declining, now at 62/54.
EKG consistently showing sinus rhythm with ST segment elevation.
No abnormal movements; patient shows no protective reflexes.
Oxygen saturation at 0 with no spontaneous breathing.
Transported in critical condition. Vitals maintained at ED entrance but declined en route.
Direct transfer to VCU Hot; ED aware of full field impression.
All clothing and personal belongings remain with the patient at the ED bed.
Minimal intervention changes after IO placement.
Likely expired from combined cerebral and cardiac trauma.
Airway stability maintained throughout field and transport.
RFD crew assisted in patient transfer and lifting for stretcher placement.
All procedures followed protocol for penetrating injuries.
Care and procedures fully documented for hospital retention.
No post-handoff field activity. Death declared by ED staff.
Handoff was uneventful and complete.
All field equipment, monitoring, and documentation transferred to hospital.
Pronounced within one hour in the ED with no change in condition post handoff.
Care completed and transferred to hospital, including equipment and documentation.
Capnography and EKG last used before ED handoff.
No response to field interventions. No movement or vital signs detected.
Vitals and waveform checked frequently during transport.
Cardiac arrest was expected and occurred shortly post handoff.
Provided full field resuscitation under severe constraints.
Patient condition fully transferred at handoff. No immediate re-intervention needed.
No exact transit time, but patient deterioration noted including lowered BP and spo2.
Stabilized in field but with low likelihood of survival estimated pre-transport.
No further secondary assessment findings to report post field injury identification.
Immobilized and no new airway, breathing interventions implemented.
Wounds include left axilla and head with clear evidence of penetrating trauma.
Transport conducted smoothly alongside ED coordination.
